title: A. S. Haulkory v Kingsmill Co Ltd
text:
A. S. Haulkory v Kingsmill Co Ltd
2026 INT 84
THE INTERMEDIATE COURT OF MAURITIUS
(Civil Division)
Cause No.: 539/2021
In the matter of:
Atmanand Singh HAULKORY
Plaintiff
v
Kingsmill Co Ltd
Defendant
RULING
By virtue of a proecipe dated 10th August 2021, Plaintiff, a freelance accountant, is
claiming Rs. 266,700/- from Defendant. The said sum represents fees due to him by
Defendant. In a plea dated 26th April 2022, Defendant, without making any admission,
explained that it appointed Plaintiff as accountant, and for reasons given therein, averred that
Plaintiff is not entitled to the said sum.
Trial has started and Plaintiff is under examination in chief. Based on Article 1341 of
the Code Civil Mauricien (CCM), learned counsel appearing for Defendant Company, Mr. Y.
Lalloo, objected to Plaintiff adducing oral evidence that he told Defendant Company’s
representative, Mr. O. Peerthy, that his hourly rate is Rs. 1,000/-, which amounted to a total of
Rs. 296,000 as per the proecipe. Learned counsel appearing for Plaintiff, Mr. A. Ramdass,
insisting on adducing this evidence, arguments were heard.
Due consideration has been given to the arguments of both Mr. Y. Lalloo and Mr. A.
Ramdass.
On the one hand, Mr. Y. Lalloo argued that the sum claimed from Defendant
Company cannot be proved ‘par tout moyen’ in light of Article 1341 of the CCM sets out
that:
“Il doit être passé acte devant notaires ou sous signatures privées de toutes choses
excédant la somme ou la valeur de cinq mille roupies, même pour dépôts volontaires;

et il n’est reçu aucune preuve par témoins contre et outre le contenu aux actes, ni sur
ce qui serait allégué avoir été dit avant, lors ou depuis les actes, encore qu’il s’agisse
d’une somme ou valeur moindre de cinq mille roupies.
Le tout sans préjudice de ce qui est prescrit dans les lois relatives au commerce.”
On the other hand, Mr. A. Ramdass argued that the Court need not rule on the matter
as a previous ruling in this case has already allowed the evidence and there already is the
evidence of Mr F. Choonee, who deposed previously on behalf of Plaintiff. Further, he argued
that in any case, Plaintiff’s activity as an accountant falls within Article 109 of the Code de
Commerce, which he submitted does not cover purely commercial acts but also “actes
mixtes”. Article 109 states that “A l'égard des commerçants, les actes de commerce peuvent
se prouver par tous moyens à moins qu’il n’en soit autrement disposé par la loi.”
The evidence of Mr. Choonee is merely that he introduced Plaintiff as an accountant
to the representative of Defendant Company, Mr. Peerthy, for tax purposes. And he was aware
that Plaintiff told the latter that his hourly rate is Rs. 1,000/-. This Court finds that a previously
ruling that Mr. Choonee’s answer is already on record is not sufficient to support Plaintiff’s
claim for the total of Rs. 266,700/-. The arguments of Mr. A. Ramdass therefore fail on that
score.
To determine whether the oral evidence of Plaintiff may be allowed, this Court must
determine whether the services which Plaintiff provided to Defendant Company is a
commercial one or not.
Both counsel made reference to the case of Dudley Grimaud v Compagnie
Mauricienne de Commerce Limitée [2023 SCJ 30], where it was held that:
“..Relying on Dalloz numérique, 2011, Preuve-Liberté en matière de Commerce, it
is the contention of plaintiff that Article 109 of the Code de Commerce does not cover purely
commercial acts but also covers “actes mixtes” as obtained in the present matter inasmuch
as “Si l’acte dont la prevue doit être rapporée a été conclu entre un commerçant et un non-
commerçant, il s’inscrit dans la catégorie des actes mixtes, parce qu’il n’est commercial que
pour l’une des parties .... Dès lors, la liberté de la preuve n’est opposable qu’à la partie pour
laquelle l’acte a un caractère commercial, c’est à dire au commerçant.”...
..True it is that if the “acte” had been one which is “mixte”, there would have been no
need for the “non-commerçant” to have produced an “écrit” against the “commerçant”...”
From Plaintiff’s evidence and documents produced, Plaintiff is an accountant who
offered his services to Defendant Company as such. Accounting services are not specifically
2

mentioned in Articles 1 to 3 of the Code de Commerce, where traditional commercial
transactions have been listed.
Mr. Y. Lalloo contends that Plaintiff is not a “commerçant” and that Defendant
Company’s transaction with Plaintiff is not a commercial one as the accountancy services is
not within the realm of Defendant Company’s business. It must however be noted at this stage
of proceedings, that is no evidence on record or in the nature of Defendant Company’s activity,
although this Court notes that both learned counsel mentioned bakery in their arguments.
Mr. A. Ramdass contends that Plaintiff is a “non-commerçant” who is not precluded
from adducing oral evidence of a commercial transaction which he had with Defendant
Company which is a “commerçant”, pursuant to Grimaud (supra).
In the present case, an analogy can be drawn to the case of G. Runghen v P.
Runghen [1935 MR 38] where Plaintiff, an accountant, sought to be paid for work he did for
his father, Defendant, at the latter’s shop. The Court in Runghen (supra) relied on
Buckmuller v Maurel [1862 MR 147], where it was held:
“This is a practical question of importance on which considerable diversity of authority
is found to exist. It is proper to have it settled. We agree with the writers who are of opinion
that the hiring of the services of a clerk by a merchant for the use solely of his business is an
act of commerce, cognisable by commercial tribunals, and may accordingly be established by
parole proof...
...In the present suit, looking at the admitted position of the parties, as shopkeeper and
clerk, and the steady inclination of later times to relax the rigid rules of exclusion of evidence,
particularly in commercial matter, we think this exposition of the law is to be preferred. And
we shall therefore allow the plaintiff’s witness to be heard.”
In Runghen (supra), reference was made to the following:
“Lacour et Bouteron, Précis de Droit Commercial, I. Note 760.
.. L'art. 109 ne vise textuellement que les “achats et ventes” Cependant l'admissibilité de la
preuve testimoniale devant la juridiction consulaire était une règle générale dans l’ancien
droit.; et le code civil lui-même, art. 1341, apporte aux règles posées dans cet article une
exception pour les matières commerciales... Aussi est-on d’accord pour admettre que la règle
est générale et concerne tous les contrats commerciaux (sauf ceux pour lesquels le Code
de Commerce exige exceptionnellement un écrit). La loi a parlé de la vente, parce que c'est
le type le plus usuel de ces contrats.
Dalloz, Rep. Prat. Vo. Louage d'ouvrage, Note 101.
Lorsque le contrat de louage de services est commercial, tous les modes de preuve admis en
matière commerciale (C. Com. 109) sont recevables. S'il n'a ce caractère qu'à l'égard d’une
3

des parties, c’est contre elle seulement que la preuve peut être ainsi administrée; et cette
partie, vis-à-vis de son co-contractant, doit, pour la preuve qui lui incombe, suivre les règles
du droit civil.
Note 102: -Le contrat de louage de services formé entre un industriel ou un commerçant et
ses ouvriers ou employés est civil au regard de ces derniers et commercial au regard du
maître.
D.P. 1912.1.304: -
Les contrats de louage de services entre un commerçant et ses ouvriers on préposés, étant
pour le premier des actes de commerce, sont, à son égard, soumis, quant à la preuve de leur
existence et de leurs conditions, aux règles du droit commercial.
Ils peuvent donc être prouvés par témoins et par présomptions.
Vide also Lyon-Caen, 5th Edn., Vol. I pp. 194 and 195.”
The Court in Runghen (supra) exercised its discretion in favour of Plaintiff whom it
allowed to prove by oral evidence his contract of “louage de services” as accountant to the
defendant.
In the present case, from the evidence adduced so far, Plaintiff was introduced by one
of his clients to the representative of Defendant Company in relation to a tax issue. Applying
the reasoning in Runghen(supra), his transaction with Defendant Company falls under
“louage de services” thus falling under Article 109 of the Code de Commerce. His oral
evidence is therefore allowed, in support of the pleadings as averred in the proecipe, since
this Court is bound by pleadings.
K. Poollay Mootien
Magistrate
9th April 2026
4
